easy.title: Build a 'Famous Quotes' Collection Page
easy.description: Create an HTML page that displays a collection of 5 famous quotes. Use <blockquote> for longer quotes and <q> for shorter quotes. Include proper citation for each using <cite> and the cite attribute. One quote must use nested <q> elements. Display abbreviations like e.g. and i.e. using <abbr> with title attributes. Include an <address> at the bottom with your contact info.
easy.starterCode:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>Famous Quotes</title>
</head>
<body>
    <!-- Build a quotes collection page -->
</body>
</html>
easy.solution:
Famous Quotes Collection

A collection of inspiring quotes from remarkable individuals.

Inspirational Quotes

Your work is going to fill a large part of your life, and the only way to be truly satisfied is to do what you believe is great work. And the only way to do great work is to love what you do.

Steve Jobs, Stanford Commencement Speech

Imagination is more important than knowledge. Knowledge is limited. Imagination encircles the world.

Albert Einstein

Short Quotes

As Dr. Seuss said, Oh, the places you'll go!

He remarked, She told me, I can do this, and I believed her.

Remember: It does not matter how slowly you go as long as you do not stop.

About This Page

etc. — more quotes will be added regularly.

Curated by Jane Doe
Email: jane@example.com
Follow on Social Media

© 2026 Famous Quotes Collection

medium.title: Build an Article with Pull Quotes and Citations
medium.description: Write a short article (300+ words) on a topic of your choice. Include: at least 4 <blockquote> elements with proper citations, several <q> elements for inline quotes, <abbr> elements for abbreviations, an <address> with article author info, and one <aside> containing a pull quote. Use the cite attribute on all quotes. Mark up the article title with <cite> in the references section.
medium.starterCode:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>Article with Pull Quotes</title>
</head>
<body>
    <!-- Write an article with proper quotation markup -->
</body>
</html>
medium.solution:
The Importance of Web Accessibility

By Mr. John Smith | June 12, 2026

Web accessibility is not an optional feature — it is a fundamental requirement for an inclusive internet. As W3C states, The power of the Web is in its universality.

Consider the statistics: over one billion people worldwide have some form of disability. As Dr. Sarah Chen notes in her research:

When we design for accessibility, we create better experiences for everyone. The curb-cut effect shows that solutions designed for disabled users often benefit all users.

Dr. Sarah Chen, Inclusive Design Principles

Many organizations are recognizing this. The ADA has seen increased enforcement in digital spaces. A judge recently ruled, Websites are places of public accommodation, setting an important precedent.

Key Insight

Accessibility is not charity. It is good business.

Common Misconceptions

Some developers believe that accessibility is too expensive or time-consuming. As the WAI explains:

Integrating accessibility from the start of a project adds minimal cost — often less than 5% of the total budget. Retrofitting accessibility later can increase costs significantly.

WAI, Accessibility Business Case

One developer told me, I thought accessibility was just about screen readers. I was wrong. It is about SEO, mobile users, and everyone else.

Getting Started

The first step is education. As accessibility advocate Helen Miller says:

Start with semantic HTML. Use proper heading hierarchy, add alt text to images, and ensure sufficient color contrast. These basic steps cover 80% of accessibility issues.

Helen Miller, Accessibility First Conference

Remember: The web is for everyone. Let us build it that way.

References

  • Inclusive Design Principles by Dr. Sarah Chen
  • Web Accessibility Initiative (WAI) documentation
  • Accessibility First Conference keynote address
About the Author
John Smith is a web developer and accessibility advocate.
Contact: john@example.com
@johnsmith

© 2026 John Smith. All rights reserved.

hard.title: Build a Research Paper with Full Citation System
hard.description: Create an academic-style research paper page that demonstrates advanced quotation and citation markup. Include: a <blockquote> for each source cited, with proper cite attribute and nested <cite> elements, <q> for inline citations, a references section using <cite> for all works cited, <abbr> for all acronyms and abbreviations, a footnotes section using <sup> for superscript references, and a semantic <address> for author info. Use bidirectional elements <bdi> for foreign language quotes and <bdo> for a short right-to-left quote.
hard.starterCode:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>Research Paper: Semantic Quotation in HTML</title>
</head>
<body>
    <!-- Build an academic research paper with full citation system -->
</body>
</html>
hard.solution:
Semantic Quotation in HTML: A Comprehensive Analysis

Author: Dr. Maria Rodriguez
Affiliation: UWT
Email: m.rodriguez@uwt.edu
Date: June 12, 2026

Abstract

This paper examines the semantic quotation elements available in HTML, analyzing their proper usage, accessibility implications, and best practices for modern web development.

1. Introduction

The W3C has long advocated for semantic markup. As Berners-Lee[1] originally stated, I want the Web to be a place where anything can be connected to anything. This vision extends to how we mark up quotations.

Previous research[2] has shown that semantic quotation markup improves accessibility for screen reader users. One user reported, When reading a blockquote, my screen reader announces the quotation, which helps me understand the context.

2. The <q> Element

The <q> element is designed for short, inline quotations. As the WHATWG specification[3] explains:

The q element represents some phrasing content quoted from another source. Browsers should render quotation marks around the content.

HTML Living Standard, WHATWG

Nested quotations are handled gracefully, with browsers alternating styles. For example: The witness stated, I heard him say, I will not go, and then he left.

3. The <blockquote> Element

For longer quotations, <blockquote> is the appropriate element. According to the MDN documentation[4]:

The <blockquote> element indicates that the enclosed text is an extended quotation. Usually, this is rendered visually by indentation.

MDN Web Docs

The element can contain complex content, including multiple paragraphs and citations:

Semantic HTML is the foundation of accessible web content. Without proper markup, assistive technologies cannot interpret the structure and meaning of web pages.

Developers must prioritize semantic elements to ensure their content is universally accessible.

Johnson & Lee, Web Accessibility Research (2025)

4. Multilingual Considerations

For quotations in different languages, use the lang attribute. For example, a French quote: C'est la vie. For text with different directionality:

User names like أحمد محمد should be isolated with the <bdi> element to prevent direction changes from affecting surrounding text. For forced direction override:

This text is rendered right-to-left.

5. Accessibility Analysis

Screen reader testing[5] reveals that semantic quotation elements significantly improve the browsing experience:

Users navigating with screen readers reported 40% faster comprehension of quoted material when semantic elements were used instead of visual quotation marks.

Accessibility Research Group, Screen Reader Usability Study

6. Conclusion

Semantic quotation elements are essential tools for creating accessible, meaningful web content. As Dr. Williams noted, The difference between semantic and presentational markup is the difference between a library and a pile of books.

Footnotes

  1. Berners-Lee, T. (1999). Weaving the Web. HarperCollins.
  2. Chen, S. (2025). Accessibility Impact of Semantic HTML. JWA, 12(3), 45-67.
  3. HTML Living Standard. WHATWG. https://html.spec.whatwg.org/
  4. MDN Web Docs: <blockquote>. Mozilla. https://developer.mozilla.org/
  5. Johnson, P. & Lee, M. (2026). Screen Reader Usability with Semantic Markup. UXR Conference Proceedings.
